text: God. This prayer, which continues to the end of the book, is a paraphrase of 3 Kings 3:6., etc., with several additional instructions, to remind people in authority of the love of justice. (Calmet)

King. Hence it appears, that the sentiments of Solomon are expressed. (Worthington)<hr>Judge. This is the first duty of a king.<hr>Thy sons. He ought to govern his people, remembering that they are God's creatures and children.

Beginning. In thy eternal decrees, or when thou madest choice of Israel. (Haydock)<hr>The temple was greater, but very like the tabernacle: only the court of the priests was divided from that of the people. In the desert, the whole camp was considered a the court of the latter. (Calmet)

With thee. The uncreated wisdom is God himself: that which is communicated to man is his gift, ver. 10. (Worthington)<hr>World. Proverbs 8:22. This request shews what idea Solomon had of God's majesty, for which he was preparing a temple.
Thought. How shall we govern as we ought, and act as thy vicegerents, without thy Spirit? (Proverbs 16:10., and 2 Kings 14:17.)

Whosoever, etc., is not in the Septuagint. But men includes as much. (Haydock)<hr>The saints have been preserved from the contagion and punishments of the world only by means of the divine benefit. (Calmet)
